sections.summary: Document title and introductory information: Standard for Cape Gooseberry (CXS 226-2001), adopted 2001 and amended 2005 and 2011, with common regional names listed—non-substantive introductory material.

Standard applies to commercial varieties of cape gooseberries (Physalis peruviana) supplied fresh to the consumer after preparation and packaging, and excludes cape gooseberries intended for industrial processing.

Minimum quality requirements require cape gooseberries to be whole (with or without calyx), sound, clean, practically free of pests and pest damage, free of abnormal external moisture and foreign smell/taste, firm, fresh in appearance and with smooth shiny skin (peduncle ≤25 mm); maturity must allow transport and satisfactory arrival and be confirmed by colour/total soluble solids (minimum 14.0°Brix), and fruit are classified into Extra, Class I and Class II with progressively relaxed allowances for slight defects that must not affect the pulp.

In all classes cape gooseberries must be whole (with or without calyx), sound, clean, practically free of pests and pest damage, free of abnormal external moisture and foreign smell/taste, firm, fresh in appearance, with smooth shiny skin, and if calyx present the peduncle must not exceed 25 mm.

sections.section: 2.1.1 The cape gooseberries must have reached an appropriate degree of development and ripeness account being taken of the characteristics of the variety and the area in which they are grown.
sections.summary: Cape gooseberries must have reached an appropriate degree of development and ripeness for the variety and growing area and be in a condition to withstand transport and handling and arrive in satisfactory condition at destination.
Maturity is assessed visually by external colour (green to orange) and confirmed by total soluble solids, calyx colour is not indicative of ripeness, and soluble solids must be at least 14.0°Brix.

Cape gooseberries are classified into three classes—'Extra' (superior, essentially defect‑free), Class I (good quality allowing slight shape, colour or skin defects) and Class II (meets minimum requirements but may have more defects, including small healed cracks up to 5% of surface)—in all cases defects must not affect the pulp, and classification is independent of size and colour.

'Extra' class: cape gooseberries must be of superior quality, characteristic of the variety/commercial type, and free of defects except very slight superficial ones that do not affect appearance, quality, keeping quality or presentation.

Class I: cape gooseberries must be of good quality and characteristic of the variety/commercial type, with only slight defects in shape, colouring or skin allowed provided they do not affect appearance, quality, keeping quality, presentation or the pulp.

Class II: includes fruit that meet minimum requirements but not higher class criteria, allowing defects in shape, colouring and skin and small healed cracks not exceeding 5% of total surface area, provided defects do not affect the pulp.
Size is determined by the maximum equatorial diameter with a minimum of 15 mm and assigned to size codes A–D as follows: A 15.0–18.0 mm, B 18.1–20.0 mm, C 20.1–22.0 mm, D >22.1 mm.

Tolerances allow percentages of non-conforming fruit by class (Extra 5% may meet Class I, Class I 10% may meet Class II, Class II 10% may fail class/minimum except severely damaged with up to 20% allowed for fruit with healed cracks >5%), and size tolerances permit 10% by number or weight to be the size immediately above and/or below that indicated on the package.

Quality tolerances by class: Extra—5% by number/weight may not meet Extra but must meet Class I (or its tolerances); Class I—10% may not meet Class I but must meet Class II (or its tolerances); Class II—10% may satisfy neither the class nor minimum requirements (excluding severely damaged fruit) and up to 20% with small healed cracks >5% surface is accepted.
'Extra' class tolerance: up to 5% by number or weight of fruit may not satisfy 'Extra' requirements but must meet Class I or, exceptionally, fall within Class I tolerances.
Class I tolerance: up to 10% by number or weight of fruit may not satisfy Class I requirements but must meet Class II or, exceptionally, fall within Class II tolerances.

Class II tolerance: up to 10% by number or weight may satisfy neither the class nor minimum requirements (excluding fruit unfit for consumption), and up to 20% by number or weight of fruit with small healed cracks covering more than 5% of surface is accepted.
Size tolerances: for all classes, 10% by number or weight of cape gooseberries may correspond to the size immediately above and/or below that indicated on the package.

Presentation requirements: packages must be uniform in origin, variety, quality, colouring, size and presentation with visible contents representative of the whole; produce must be packed to protect it using new, clean materials and non-toxic inks in compliance with CXC 44-1995, and containers must meet hygiene, ventilation and resistance standards and be free of foreign matter and smell.

Requires each package's contents of cape gooseberries to be uniform in origin, variety, quality, colouring, size and presentation (with or without calyx) and that the visible portion be representative of the whole.

Requires cape gooseberries to be packed to protect the produce, using new, clean, food‑grade materials that do not damage the produce and allowing printed materials only with non‑toxic ink/glue, and requires compliance with the Code of Practice for Packaging and Transport of Fresh Fruits and Vegetables (CXC 44‑1995).
Containers for cape gooseberries must meet quality, hygiene, ventilation and strength requirements to ensure proper handling, shipping and preservation, and must be free of foreign matter and odours.

Sets out marking and labelling requirements for cape gooseberries, applying the General Standard for the Labelling of Prepackaged Foods plus specific provisions for consumer and non‑retail packages (identification, nature, origin, commercial identification and optional inspection mark).
Heading stating that consumer packages must meet the General Standard for the Labelling of Prepackaged Foods (CXS 1‑1985) with additional specific provisions that follow in sub‑sections.
If cape gooseberries are not visible from outside the consumer package, the package must be labelled with the name of the produce and may include the variety or commercial type.

Non‑retail containers must bear, visibly and legibly on one side or in accompanying documents, prescribed particulars including identification, nature, origin and commercial identification, with an optional official inspection mark.
Requires the name and address of the exporter, packer and/or dispatcher to appear on non‑retail containers, with an identification code permitted as an alternative.
Requires the name of the produce on non‑retail containers if contents are not visible and permits optional labelling of the variety.
Requires the country of origin on non‑retail containers, with optional indication of district or regional/local place name.
Specifies commercial identification for non‑retail containers: class; size (size code or minimum and maximum diameter in mm); optional number of units; and optional net weight.
Indicates that an official inspection mark may be applied to non‑retail containers (optional), with no further requirements in this subsection.
Requires the produce to comply with the Codex General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193‑1995) and with Codex maximum residue limits for pesticides.

sections.section: 7.2 The produce covered by this Standard shall comply with the maximum residue limits for pesticides established by the Codex Alimentarius Commission.
sections.summary: States that the produce must comply with pesticide maximum residue limits established by the Codex Alimentarius Commission.

Recommends preparing and handling the produce in accordance with Codex hygiene texts (General Principles of Food Hygiene, Code of Hygienic Practice for Fresh Fruits and Vegetables, etc.) and complying with any established microbiological criteria.

sections.section: 8.1 It is recommended that the produce covered by the provisions of this Standard be prepared and handled in accordance with the appropriate sections of the General Principles of Food Hygiene (CXC 1-1969), Code of Hygienic Practice for Fresh Fruits and Vegetables (CXC 53-2003), and other relevant Codex texts such as Codes of Hygienic Practice and Codes of Practice.
sections.summary: Recommends that the produce be prepared and handled following the General Principles of Food Hygiene (CXC 1‑1969), the Code of Hygienic Practice for Fresh Fruits and Vegetables (CXC 53‑2003) and other relevant Codex texts.

sections.section: 8.2 The produce should comply with any microbiological criteria established in accordance with the Principles and Guidelines for the Establishment and Application of Microbiological Criteria Related to Foods (CXG 21-1997).
sections.summary: States that the produce should comply with microbiological criteria established according to the Principles and Guidelines for the Establishment and Application of Microbiological Criteria Related to Foods (CXG 21‑1997).
